news_summary: Recent advances in health include breakthroughs in Alzheimer's treatment and genetic insights into borderline personality disorder, improved access to lifesaving care in Yemen, and the development of a more comfortable mineral sunscreen.
news_summary_fr: Parmi les avancées récentes dans le domaine de la santé, on peut citer des percées dans le traitement de la maladie d'Alzheimer et de nouvelles découvertes génétiques concernant le trouble de la personnalité limite, un meilleur accès aux soins vitaux au Yémen, ainsi que la mise au point d'un écran solaire minéral plus confortable.
news_summary_es: Entre los avances recientes en el ámbito de la salud destacan los avances en el tratamiento del Alzheimer y los descubrimientos genéticos sobre el trastorno límite de la personalidad, la mejora del acceso a la atención sanitaria vital en Yemen y el desarrollo de un protector solar mineral más cómodo.

articles.title: Alzheimer’s breakthrough: Scientists restore two hours of sleep without clearing brain plaques
articles.summary: Researchers have uncovered a surprising culprit behind sleep loss in Alzheimer’s disease: the brain’s own immune cells. In mice with amyloid plaques, overactive microglia triggered inflammation that kept the brain from getting enough deep, restorative sleep. Temporarily removing most of these cells restored more than two hours of sleep per day, even though the plaques remained unchanged.
articles.content:
Imagine a small fire breaking out in one corner of your kitchen. With the right extinguisher, you might be able to stop it quickly. Instead, the sprinkler system activates and floods the entire house, turning a contained problem into widespread damage.

Researchers say something similar may happen in the brains of people with Alzheimer's disease. Amyloid plaques, sticky clumps of protein that accumulate in the brain, are like the fire. Microglia, the brain's resident immune cells, act like the sprinklers. Their response is meant to protect the brain, but it may ultimately make the situation worse.

A team at the University of Kentucky has now identified this damaging process for the first time and demonstrated a way to shut it down.

Brain Immune Cells Linked to Alzheimer's Sleep Loss

In a study published in the journal Alzheimer's & Dementia, researchers led by Shannon L. Macauley, Ph.D., an associate professor of physiology in the UK College of Medicine, and first author Nicholas J. Constantino, Ph.D., a recent UK doctoral graduate, found that microglia are the main drivers of sleep loss in an animal model of Alzheimer's.

When the researchers used a drug to temporarily eliminate most of these cells, the animals regained more than two hours of sleep each day. The result points to a potential new treatment target for the disease, which Macauley called "paradigm shifting."

Scientists had previously attributed Alzheimer's related sleep loss mainly to damaged neurons or the physical presence of amyloid plaques. The new findings suggest that the disruption may instead come from a much broader immune reaction, similar to a "whole house response."

"Basically, we showed that it is not the plaques themselves, or solely dysfunctional neurons, that cause sleep loss but actually microglia," said Macauley. "Microglia are immune cells that, when they respond to plaques, kick off this elaborate cascade of inflammation, as if the microglia are partying all night, and keeping the brain awake."

Tracking Sleep and Brain Activity

To separate Alzheimer's related changes from those caused by normal aging, the researchers studied two groups of mice. One group had a genetic tendency to develop amyloid plaques, while the other consisted of "wild-type" mice that aged normally.

The animals were examined at six months of age, when plaques begin to appear, and again at 18 months, a stage representing advanced disease.

The team used several advanced tools to closely monitor changes in sleep and brain activity. The mice wore small head mounted devices that recorded electroencephalography (EEG) and electromyography (EMG).

An EEG captures patterns of electrical activity and oscillations across brain networks, creating what can be thought of as an electrical fingerprint of the brain. An EMG measures muscle activity. Together, the two methods allowed researchers to determine precisely when the animals were awake, in deep restorative sleep or in dreaming sleep.

To locate the immune cells that were "partying all night," as Macauley described them, the researchers also used a technique called light sheet microscopy. The method makes brain tissue transparent, then uses a thin plane of laser light to build a detailed 3D digital image.

This approach gave the team a full view of both amyloid plaques and immune cells throughout the brain.

Temporarily Removing Microglia

To test whether microglia were actually causing the sleep disruption, the scientists used a drug called Pexidartinib (PLX3397). The medication was first developed for cancer research and blocks a signaling pathway that microglia depend on for survival.

After the mice received the drug for 14 days, about 87% of the brain's immune cells were temporarily removed. The researchers could then determine whether sleep improved in their absence.

The team also used a mathematical method called Fitting Oscillations and One Over Frequency to divide the brain's electrical activity into two categories: periodic activity (the rhythmic waves we usually think of as brain waves) and aperiodic activity (the background electrical noise).

Using a car comparison, the researchers were effectively testing whether the brain's engine continued to run at an unusually high speed even while the animals were resting.

Early Plaques Trigger a Lasting Sleep Deficit

Macauley called the results "mind-blowing and unexpected." The findings showed that plaque accumulation and sleep disruption did not worsen together in a steady decline.

"I expected that as plaque burden became more severe, sleep disruption would also worsen," said Constantino. "The disruptions in sleep and cortical EEG activity that occur at six months, when plaques first emerge, did not worsen by 18 months, despite more than double the amount of plaque burden."

The team described this pattern as a ceiling effect. Even though plaque levels more than doubled, the amount of lost sleep remained about the same.

That result suggests that the first wave of immune activity triggered by early plaques may be enough to establish the sleep problem. Additional plaques may not produce a proportional increase in sleep disruption.

Alzheimer's Targets Restorative Sleep

The study also helped distinguish the effects of normal aging from those linked to Alzheimer's pathology.

Normal aging primarily reduced rapid eye movement (REM) sleep, the stage associated with dreaming and memory consolidation. In contrast, amyloid pathology selectively reduced nonrapid eye movement (NREM) sleep, the deeply restorative stage of sleep.

"That restorative sleep is super important for physical repair, learning and memory and washing out the toxins of the day," Macauley said. "When Alzheimer's patients lose this stage, they lose their brain's primary cleaning cycle, creating a feed-forward loop that may drive further damage."

The loss of this restorative period could therefore create a harmful cycle. Poor sleep may reduce the brain's ability to clear waste, which could contribute to further damage and even more sleep disruption.

More Than Two Hours of Sleep Restored

The most striking result came after the researchers depleted the microglia.

Mice with Alzheimer's related pathology gained more than two hours of sleep per night once most of their brain immune cells had been removed. Their periods of restorative NREM sleep also became longer, giving them more opportunities to enter healthy dreaming sleep that supports the formation of new memories.

Importantly, the improvement occurred even though the amount of amyloid plaque in the brain did not change.

This finding suggests that the inflammatory response to plaques may be a reversible cause of sleep loss and could potentially be treated separately from the plaques themselves.

It also raises a major question for future research: could restoring this essential sleep in people help interrupt the feed-forward loop associated with Alzheimer's disease?

A Collaborative Lab Culture

The discovery grew out of the research environment in Macauley's laboratory in the Department of Physiology at the Sanders-Brown Center on Aging. Macauley credited the progress to a "beautiful partnership" between herself, her students and other trainees.

"I love people who take initiative, find their passion are curious and keep pushing to find an answer," Macauley said.

She encourages members of her team to become "calculated risk-takers," and keeps a Wayne Gretzky quote in her office: "You miss 100% of the shots you don't take."

Constantino, who recently defended his doctorate at UK, said that this atmosphere gave him the confidence to pursue challenging questions that crossed multiple fields.

"Dr. Macauley has also taught me to embrace uncertainty and failure as part of the scientific process," Constantino said. "Some of the most interesting studies I have been a part of emerged because our original hypothesis was wrong."

When experiments encounter obstacles, Macauley encourages the team to keep investigating rather than stop.

"Follow the data, ask better questions and figure out what is actually happening."

That approach helped the researchers move beyond the field's traditional focus on neurons and investigate whether microglia could be a treatment target.

Portable EEG Could Aid Earlier Detection

The broader goal of the research is to develop affordable and noninvasive tools for people affected by Alzheimer's disease. The current findings provide several directions for future work.

The researchers identified patterns of electrical brain activity that appear to distinguish Alzheimer's related changes from normal aging. They believe EEG technology could eventually serve as a "readily accessible, affordable and longitudinal biomarker of Alzheimer's disease."

"Portable EEG systems could allow us to monitor people in their home environments and potentially screen for changes associated with an Alzheimer's disease, without the initial need for expensive or invasive tests," Macauley said.

Such devices could potentially allow local clinics across Kentucky to screen people at risk before requiring them to travel long distances to major hospitals for more specialized testing.

Calming Microglia Without Removing Them

Macauley's laboratory is now studying ways to reduce microglial overactivity without eliminating the cells completely.

The team is examining safe medications that are already in use, including the diabetes drug Metformin and the antiseizure drug Stiripentol. Researchers want to determine whether these medicines can change how microglia process energy and reduce their tendency to become overactive.

By preventing immune cells from keeping the brain's engine in a heightened state, the team hopes to restore healthy sleep and improve quality of life years before noticeable memory loss develops.

"If we can target that process, it might help with quality of life, attention, cognition and confusion," Macauley said.

Finding effective solutions begins with identifying both the source of the problem and the right tool to address it. Macauley's team is making progress on both fronts.

Research reported in this publication was supported by the National Institute on Aging of the National Institutes of Health under Award Numbers R01AG068330, R01AG093847 and P30AG072946, and by the National Institute of General Medical Sciences of the National Institutes of Health under Award Numbers P30GM127211 and P20GM148326. The content is solely the responsibility of the authors and does not necessarily represent the official views of the National Institutes of Health.

This work was supported by a $287,236 award from the Cure Alzheimer's Fund.

This work was supported by a $250,000 award from The CART Fund (Coins for Alzheimer's Research Trust).
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in understanding and potentially treating sleep loss in Alzheimer's disease by targeting brain immune cells (microglia). This discovery offers a new therapeutic avenue that could improve sleep and quality of life for many Alzheimer's patients, addressing a major health challenge with broad societal impact. The article provides detailed context, experimental methods, and implications, fulfilling the criteria for inspiring good news.
articles.personality_title: Scientists restore over two hours of sleep by calming brain immune cells in Alzheimer's mice
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Alzheimer's disease causes memory problems and other brain issues. One common problem is poor sleep, especially losing deep, restorative sleep that helps the brain clean itself and work well. Scientists thought this sleep loss was mainly due to sticky protein clumps called amyloid plaques building up in the brain.

**What happened** – Researchers at the University of Kentucky studied mice with Alzheimer's-like plaques. They found that brain immune cells called microglia become overactive and cause inflammation, which stops the brain from getting enough deep sleep. When scientists used a drug to temporarily remove most microglia, the mice gained more than two extra hours of sleep each day—even though the plaques stayed the same.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it shows that sleep problems in Alzheimer's might come from the brain’s immune response, not just the plaques themselves. By calming or reducing microglia activity, we might improve sleep and brain health without having to remove plaques. Better sleep could help slow down brain damage and improve quality of life for people with Alzheimer's.

**What's next step** – The researchers want to find safe medicines that can calm microglia without removing them completely. They are testing existing drugs like Metformin and Stiripentol to see if these can reduce microglia's overactivity. They also hope to develop portable brain activity monitors (EEG devices) to detect Alzheimer's-related changes early and track treatment effects at home.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Targeting the brain’s immune cells can restore lost sleep in Alzheimer’s disease, opening new paths for treatment and better brain health.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques restaurent plus de deux heures de sommeil en calmant les cellules immunitaires cérébrales chez des souris atteintes d’Alzheimer
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La maladie d’Alzheimer provoque des problèmes de mémoire et d’autres troubles cérébraux. Un problème fréquent est la mauvaise qualité du sommeil, notamment la perte du sommeil profond et réparateur qui aide le cerveau à se nettoyer et à bien fonctionner. Jusqu’ici, on pensait que cette perte de sommeil était surtout due à l’accumulation de plaques amyloïdes, des amas de protéines collantes dans le cerveau.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université du Kentucky ont étudié des souris avec des plaques similaires à celles d’Alzheimer. Ils ont découvert que des cellules immunitaires du cerveau, appelées microglies, deviennent trop actives et provoquent une inflammation qui empêche le cerveau d’avoir assez de sommeil profond. En utilisant un médicament pour éliminer temporairement la plupart des microglies, les souris ont retrouvé plus de deux heures de sommeil par jour, même si les plaques restaient présentes.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle montre que les problèmes de sommeil dans Alzheimer viennent peut-être de la réponse immunitaire du cerveau, et pas seulement des plaques. En calmant ou en réduisant l’activité des microglies, on pourrait améliorer le sommeil et la santé du cerveau sans enlever les plaques. Un meilleur sommeil pourrait ralentir les dégâts cérébraux et améliorer la vie des personnes atteintes.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs veulent trouver des médicaments sûrs pour calmer les microglies sans les supprimer complètement. Ils testent des médicaments déjà utilisés comme la Metformine et le Stiripentol pour voir s’ils peuvent réduire l’activité excessive des microglies. Ils espèrent aussi développer des appareils EEG portables pour détecter tôt les changements liés à Alzheimer et suivre les traitements à domicile.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Cibler les cellules immunitaires du cerveau peut restaurer le sommeil perdu dans la maladie d’Alzheimer, ouvrant de nouvelles pistes pour traiter et protéger le cerveau.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos restauran más de dos horas de sueño al calmar células inmunitarias cerebrales en ratones con Alzheimer
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La enfermedad de Alzheimer causa problemas de memoria y otros daños en el cerebro. Un problema común es la mala calidad del sueño, especialmente la pérdida del sueño profundo y reparador que ayuda al cerebro a limpiarse y funcionar bien. Se pensaba que esta pérdida de sueño se debía principalmente a la acumulación de placas amiloides, grupos pegajosos de proteínas en el cerebro.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Kentucky estudiaron ratones con placas similares a las del Alzheimer. Descubrieron que las células inmunitarias del cerebro llamadas microglías se vuelven demasiado activas y causan inflamación, lo que impide que el cerebro tenga suficiente sueño profundo. Cuando usaron un medicamento para eliminar temporalmente la mayoría de las microglías, los ratones recuperaron más de dos horas adicionales de sueño al día, aunque las placas permanecieron igual.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo es importante porque muestra que los problemas de sueño en Alzheimer podrían venir de la respuesta inmunitaria del cerebro, no solo de las placas. Al calmar o reducir la actividad de las microglías, podríamos mejorar el sueño y la salud cerebral sin tener que eliminar las placas. Un mejor sueño podría ayudar a frenar el daño cerebral y mejorar la calidad de vida.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores quieren encontrar medicamentos seguros que calmen las microglías sin eliminarlas por completo. Están probando fármacos existentes como Metformina y Stiripentol para ver si pueden reducir la actividad excesiva de las microglías. También esperan desarrollar dispositivos portátiles de EEG para detectar temprano cambios relacionados con Alzheimer y seguir el tratamiento en casa.

**Frase clave** – Apuntar a las células inmunitarias del cerebro puede restaurar el sueño perdido en la enfermedad de Alzheimer, abriendo nuevas vías para el tratamiento y la salud cerebral.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a serene brain-shaped landscape at dusk, where gentle, glowing microglia-like lanterns peacefully dim their light around soft, amber amyloid plaque-like clusters, while a calm mouse silhouette rests beneath a cozy blanket of rhythmic, wave-like patterns symbolizing restored deep sleep.

articles.title: Health response brings lifesaving care closer to thousands in Yemen
articles.summary: Thousands of people in some of Yemen's most conflict-affected communities are receiving lifesaving health care closer to home, thanks to funding from the UN aid coordination office (OCHA) through the Yemen Humanitarian Fund.
articles.content:
The support, implemented by the World Health Organization (WHO), has expanded access to emergency surgery, maternal and child health services, outbreak response and treatment for chronic diseases in four hard-to-reach districts where years of conflict have left health systems severely weakened.

More than 42,000 people, including internally displaced families, women, children and people living with chronic illnesses, have benefited from the year-long initiative in Abyan, Al Bayda, Al Husha and Far' Al Udayn.

Tweet URL

Emergency response

For people injured by conflict or accidents, access to emergency surgery can determine whether they live or die.

Between August 2025 and February 2026, WHO-supported surgical teams carried out 864 operations at hospitals in Al Bayda, Far' Al Udayn and Lawdar, nearly double the original target of 462 procedures.

The programme also supplied hospitals with essential medicines and trauma equipment, helping frontline health workers continue treating patients despite limited resources.

Yemen also continues to battle recurring outbreaks of cholera, measles and other infectious diseases, placing already vulnerable communities at greater risk.

With OCHA funding, WHO equipped health facilities with emergency medicines, oral rehydration salts, intravenous fluids and rapid response kits.

Health teams carried out 207 field missions to investigate suspected outbreaks, trace contacts and contain the spread of disease, exceeding the project's original target and helping reduce the risk of wider outbreaks.

Providing support

The programme also helped sustain maternal and child health services at two hospitals serving vulnerable populations.

During the reporting period, health workers provided more than 14,000 obstetric and gynaecological consultations, over 4,600 antenatal visits and more than 12,000 paediatric consultations.

They also supported nearly 2,700 deliveries, including Caesarean sections.

No maternal deaths were recorded at the supported facilities during the reporting period, helping ensure safer pregnancies and healthier starts for newborns.

The initiative also ensured continuity of care for people living with diabetes, hypertension and cardiovascular disease by supplying essential medicines to four health facilities.

More than 5,200 patients received treatment, helping prevent interruptions in care that can lead to serious complications or death.

Continued support needed

Dr. Syed Jaffar Hussain, WHO Representative in Yemen, said OCHA's support through the Yemen Humanitarian Fund has been instrumental in ensuring vulnerable communities can continue accessing lifesaving health services despite the country's ongoing humanitarian crisis.

"Through this partnership, WHO has been able to strengthen emergency and trauma care, support outbreak response, sustain maternal and child health services and ensure continuity of care for people living with chronic diseases” he said.

As humanitarian needs continue to grow across Yemen, sustained international support will be essential to ensure vulnerable communities can continue accessing the care they urgently need.
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant, real-world health intervention in conflict-affected Yemen, expanding access to emergency surgery, maternal and child health, outbreak response, and chronic disease treatment for over 42,000 vulnerable people. The initiative has measurable positive impacts including doubled surgical operations, no maternal deaths in supported facilities, and effective outbreak containment, demonstrating broad and meaningful benefits to a large population in a humanitarian crisis.
articles.personality_title: Lifesaving health care reaches thousands in Yemen’s conflict zones
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Yemen has been facing a long and difficult conflict that has damaged many hospitals and health services. People living in remote and war-affected areas often find it hard to get the medical care they need.

**What happened** – With funding from the United Nations Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs (OCHA) through the Yemen Humanitarian Fund, the World Health Organization (WHO) helped bring important health services closer to people in four hard-to-reach districts. From August 2025 to February 2026, surgical teams performed 864 emergency operations, nearly twice the planned number. The program also supported mothers and children by providing thousands of medical consultations and assisted nearly 2,700 births without any maternal deaths. Health workers responded to disease outbreaks and treated over 5,200 patients with chronic illnesses like diabetes and heart disease.

**Impact** – This effort improved emergency care, helped stop the spread of diseases, and kept mothers and children healthier in places where medical help was scarce. Doubling the number of surgeries and having no maternal deaths in supported hospitals show clear improvements. The program helped more than 42,000 people, including displaced families and vulnerable patients, receive life-saving treatments closer to home.

**What's next step** – The situation in Yemen remains challenging, and health needs are still very high. Continued support from international groups like OCHA and WHO will be needed to keep these vital health services running and reach even more people in need.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Thanks to international funding and WHO’s work, thousands of Yemenis in conflict zones now have better access to emergency surgery, maternal care, and disease treatment, saving many lives.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des soins vitaux rapprochés de milliers de personnes dans les zones de conflit au Yémen
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le Yémen traverse un conflit long et difficile qui a gravement endommagé de nombreux hôpitaux et services de santé. Les habitants des zones reculées et touchées par la guerre ont souvent du mal à accéder aux soins médicaux dont ils ont besoin.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Grâce au financement du Bureau des Nations Unies pour la coordination des affaires humanitaires (OCHA) via le Fonds humanitaire pour le Yémen, l’Organisation mondiale de la santé (OMS) a aidé à rapprocher des services de santé essentiels dans quatre districts difficiles d’accès. Entre août 2025 et février 2026, des équipes chirurgicales ont réalisé 864 opérations d’urgence, presque le double du nombre prévu. Le programme a aussi soutenu les mères et les enfants en fournissant des milliers de consultations médicales et en aidant près de 2 700 accouchements sans aucun décès maternel. Les équipes de santé ont répondu aux épidémies et traité plus de 5 200 patients atteints de maladies chroniques comme le diabète et les maladies cardiaques.

**Impact** – Cette initiative a amélioré les soins d’urgence, aidé à stopper la propagation des maladies et maintenu la santé des mères et des enfants dans des zones où l’aide médicale était rare. Doubler le nombre d’opérations et ne pas enregistrer de décès maternels dans les hôpitaux soutenus montrent des progrès clairs. Plus de 42 000 personnes, dont des familles déplacées et des patients vulnérables, ont bénéficié de traitements vitaux près de chez elles.

**Prochaine étape** – La situation au Yémen reste difficile et les besoins en santé sont toujours très importants. Un soutien continu des organisations internationales comme OCHA et l’OMS sera nécessaire pour maintenir ces services essentiels et toucher encore plus de personnes dans le besoin.

**Phrase clé** – Grâce au financement international et au travail de l’OMS, des milliers de Yéménites dans les zones de conflit ont désormais un meilleur accès à la chirurgie d’urgence, aux soins maternels et au traitement des maladies, sauvant ainsi de nombreuses vies.
articles.personality_title_es: Atención médica vital llega a miles en zonas de conflicto en Yemen
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Yemen enfrenta un conflicto largo y difícil que ha dañado muchos hospitales y servicios de salud. Las personas que viven en áreas remotas y afectadas por la guerra tienen dificultades para recibir la atención médica que necesitan.

**Qué pasó** – Con fondos de la Oficina de Coordinación de Asuntos Humanitarios de la ONU (OCHA) a través del Fondo Humanitario para Yemen, la Organización Mundial de la Salud (OMS) ayudó a acercar servicios médicos importantes a personas en cuatro distritos de difícil acceso. Entre agosto de 2025 y febrero de 2026, los equipos quirúrgicos realizaron 864 operaciones de emergencia, casi el doble del número planeado. El programa también apoyó a madres y niños con miles de consultas médicas y ayudó en casi 2,700 partos sin muertes maternas. Los trabajadores de salud respondieron a brotes de enfermedades y trataron a más de 5,200 pacientes con enfermedades crónicas como diabetes y problemas cardíacos.

**Impacto** – Este esfuerzo mejoró la atención de emergencia, ayudó a detener la propagación de enfermedades y mantuvo la salud de madres y niños en lugares donde la ayuda médica era escasa. El hecho de duplicar las cirugías y no registrar muertes maternas en los hospitales apoyados muestra mejoras claras. Más de 42,000 personas, incluyendo familias desplazadas y pacientes vulnerables, recibieron tratamientos que les salvaron la vida cerca de sus hogares.

**Próximo paso** – La situación en Yemen sigue siendo difícil y las necesidades de salud continúan siendo altas. Se necesitará apoyo continuo de grupos internacionales como OCHA y la OMS para mantener estos servicios médicos vitales y llegar a más personas necesitadas.

**Frase clave** – Gracias a la financiación internacional y al trabajo de la OMS, miles de yemeníes en zonas de conflicto ahora tienen mejor acceso a cirugía de emergencia, atención materna y tratamiento de enfermedades, salvando muchas vidas.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting showing a cluster of simple, sturdy medical tents and small clinics with glowing windows nestled in a rugged Yemeni landscape, surrounded by silhouettes of caring hands gently cradling symbolic representations of newborn babies, surgical instruments, and medicine bottles, all rendered in soft, natural earth tones and muted blues to evoke hope and lifesaving care amidst hardship.

articles.title: UCLA scientists develop mineral sunscreen without the chalky white cast
articles.summary: Scientists have redesigned zinc oxide particles to create a mineral sunscreen that protects against ultraviolet radiation without leaving such a noticeable white cast. The SPF 30 formula could make sunscreen easier and more appealing to use across a wider range of skin tones.
articles.content:
UCLA scientists have created a mineral sunscreen formula that leaves far less of the pale, chalky residue that often discourages people from using sun protection every day.

Dermatologists have long recommended daily sunscreen use to reduce exposure to ultraviolet radiation. Too much ultraviolet radiation is the leading preventable cause of skin cancer, which is the most common cancer in the United States.

Despite those risks, many people do not apply sunscreen regularly. One common complaint is that mineral sunscreens containing zinc oxide can leave a noticeable white or gray film on the skin.

Reshaping Zinc Oxide To Reduce White Cast

A study led by researchers at the UCLA Health Jonsson Comprehensive Cancer Center suggests that the problem could be addressed without creating an entirely new chemical ingredient. Instead, the researchers changed the physical shape of the zinc oxide particles already used in many mineral sunscreens.

The team engineered zinc oxide into microscopic four-armed structures known as tetrapods. According to the researchers, these particles provided strong protection against damaging ultraviolet radiation while producing less white cast than conventional zinc oxide formulas.

The results, published in ACS Materials Letters, could support skin cancer prevention by making mineral sunscreen more appealing and encouraging regular use among people with a wider variety of skin tones.

"This isn't just about cosmetics," said senior author of the study Paul S. Weiss, who holds a UC Presidential Chair and is a distinguished professor of chemistry & biochemistry, bioengineering, and materials science & engineering at UCLA and an investigator in the UCLA Health Jonsson Comprehensive Cancer Center. "If improving how sunscreen looks leads to more consistent use, it could have real implications for skin cancer prevention."

Why Sunscreen Appearance Matters

The potential benefits may be particularly significant for people with darker skin tones. They are often less likely to use sunscreen consistently and are more likely to receive a skin cancer diagnosis at a later stage.

Although melanoma, the deadliest form of skin cancer, is less common among people with darker skin tones, research indicates that they are considerably more likely to die from the disease. One reason is that melanoma is often discovered later, when treatment becomes more difficult.

For first author AJ Addae, a UCLA chemical biology doctoral candidate and cosmetic science entrepreneur, the research grew out of personal experience.

"I started thinking about this because I was frustrated by how mineral sunscreen looks on my own skin," said Addae. "A lot of my motivation came from my own experience trying to use mineral sunscreen and dealing with the white cast and other unsightly aesthetic issues. This led me to simply avoid sunscreen altogether. That frustration really became the starting point for this work."

Why Mineral Sunscreens Look Chalky

Zinc oxide is widely used in mineral sunscreen because it blocks UVA rays, which contribute to skin aging, as well as UVB rays, which cause sunburn and increase the risk of skin cancer. The U.S. Food and Drug Administration classifies zinc oxide as safe and effective.

Mineral sunscreens are commonly recommended for people with sensitive skin, acne-prone skin, rosacea or those who prefer non-chemical options.

However, standard zinc oxide particles often gather into clumps. This can make sunscreen formulas less stable and cause the particles to scatter visible light, producing the white or gray residue that is especially noticeable on darker skin tones.

The UCLA team investigated whether changing the particles' structure could prevent that clumping and improve the sunscreen's appearance.

Most zinc oxide used in sunscreen consists of very small, roughly spherical nanoparticles produced through chemical manufacturing methods. For the new study, the researchers examined much larger particles created through a patented high-temperature flame process. These particles take the form of tiny tetrapods.

"Because of their structure, these tetrapod-shaped particles have standoffs and form porous networks instead of collapsing into clumps," said Addae. "They can't pack tightly and aggregate, so they stay evenly distributed in the sunscreen."

SPF 30 Protection With Better Stability

The researchers compared the tetrapod-shaped zinc oxide with conventional zinc oxide nanoparticles typically found in mineral sunscreen. The tetrapod formulas delivered several practical advantages.

When the two types of zinc oxide were used at the same concentration, the sunscreen containing tetrapods reached a sun protection factor (SPF) of about 30. That level of protection is similar to what standard mineral sunscreens provide.

The tetrapod lotions also remained more stable as time passed. They showed fewer signs of separating or becoming unusually thick.

The most visible improvement involved how the particles interacted with light. In laboratory experiments and controlled applications on skin, the tetrapod sunscreen produced a warmer appearance that more closely matched natural skin tones. It did not create the same intense white or gray cast associated with conventional zinc oxide.

The researchers achieved this effect without using added pigments or specialized coatings to hide the residue.

"When I spread it on my own skin, I didn't get that white cast I usually see with zinc oxide," said Addae. "That was the moment I realized this could really work."

"What surprised us was how quickly it worked," added Weiss, who is also a member of the California NanoSystems Institute at UCLA and the UCLA Goodman-Luskin Microbiome Center. "The very first formulations already showed a visible difference."

From Materials Science to Skin Cancer Prevention

The sunscreen technology will require additional testing before it can become commercially available. Still, the researchers say the findings demonstrate how materials science could help address a practical barrier to skin cancer prevention.

"The best sunscreen is the one people will actually use," said Addae. "If zinc oxide can be made to look better on more skin tones without sacrificing protection, it could help more people protect themselves from the sun's most dangerous effects."

The researchers are now collaborating with the UCLA Health department of dermatology, including UCLA Health's Skin of Color Clinic. They plan to examine how the tetrapod particles interact with the skin microbiome and continue working toward real-world applications.

Key Takeaways

Mineral sunscreens made with zinc oxide commonly leave a visible white or chalky layer on the skin.

UCLA researchers found that changing zinc oxide particles into four-armed tetrapod shapes could reduce this effect.

In laboratory tests and controlled skin applications, the tetrapod formulas appeared warmer and closer to natural skin tones instead of producing a strong white or gray cast.

Other authors of the study are Jennifer Uyanga and Addae's thesis co-advisor professor Justin Carman of UCLA chemistry, and professor Yogendra Kumar Mishra of the University of Southern Denmark.

The study was funded in part by the National Science Foundation, the Challenge Initiative at UCLA and a Sigma Xi IFoRE Grant-in-Aid.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific advancement in mineral sunscreen formulation that reduces the white cast, a major barrier to regular sunscreen use. This innovation has broad public health implications by potentially increasing sunscreen adoption across diverse skin tones, thereby aiding skin cancer prevention. The article provides detailed context on the scientific approach, benefits, and future applications, fulfilling criteria for inspiring good news with meaningful real-world impact.
articles.personality_title: UCLA scientists create mineral sunscreen that blends better with all skin tones
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many people avoid using mineral sunscreen because it often leaves a noticeable white or gray layer on the skin. This is especially true for those with darker skin tones. Zinc oxide, a common ingredient in mineral sunscreens, blocks harmful ultraviolet (UV) rays but can clump and scatter light, causing this chalky appearance.

**What happened** – UCLA researchers redesigned zinc oxide particles by changing their shape into tiny four-armed structures called tetrapods. This new form spreads more evenly and reduces the white cast without losing protection. Their SPF 30 sunscreen formula was tested in the lab and on skin, showing a warmer color that matches natural skin tones better than regular zinc oxide sunscreens.

**Impact** – This change could make mineral sunscreen more appealing and easier to use for people with all skin colors. Since sunscreen helps prevent skin cancer, improving its look may encourage more regular use. This is important because people with darker skin often use sunscreen less and tend to be diagnosed with skin cancer at later, more dangerous stages.

**What’s next step** – The scientists will continue testing to ensure the new sunscreen is safe and effective for everyday use. They are also studying how these new particles interact with the skin’s natural bacteria. Collaborations with dermatology experts aim to bring this sunscreen closer to being available for the public.

**One-sentence takeaway** – By reshaping zinc oxide particles, UCLA scientists developed a mineral sunscreen that protects skin from the sun without leaving a visible white cast, making sun protection easier for everyone to use.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques de l'UCLA créent un écran solaire minéral qui s'adapte mieux à toutes les carnations
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Beaucoup de personnes évitent d’utiliser un écran solaire minéral car il laisse souvent une couche blanche ou grise visible sur la peau. Cela concerne particulièrement les personnes à la peau foncée. L’oxyde de zinc, ingrédient courant des écrans solaires minéraux, bloque les rayons ultraviolets (UV) nocifs mais peut s’agglutiner et réfléchir la lumière, ce qui crée cet aspect poudreux.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’UCLA ont modifié la forme des particules d’oxyde de zinc en petites structures à quatre branches appelées tétrapodes. Cette nouvelle forme se répartit mieux et réduit la couche blanche sans perdre en protection. Leur formule d’écran solaire SPF 30 a été testée en laboratoire et sur la peau, montrant une couleur plus chaude qui correspond mieux aux teints naturels que les écrans classiques.

**Impact** – Ce changement pourrait rendre l’écran solaire minéral plus attrayant et plus facile à utiliser pour toutes les carnations. Comme l’écran solaire aide à prévenir le cancer de la peau, améliorer son apparence pourrait encourager une utilisation plus régulière. C’est important car les personnes à la peau foncée utilisent souvent moins d’écran solaire et sont souvent diagnostiquées à un stade plus avancé du cancer.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont continuer à tester pour s’assurer que ce nouvel écran solaire est sûr et efficace pour un usage quotidien. Ils étudient aussi comment ces nouvelles particules interagissent avec les bactéries naturelles de la peau. Des collaborations avec des experts en dermatologie visent à rendre ce produit accessible au public.

**Résumé en une phrase** – En changeant la forme des particules d’oxyde de zinc, les scientifiques de l’UCLA ont créé un écran solaire minéral qui protège la peau du soleil sans laisser de trace blanche visible, facilitant ainsi la protection solaire pour tous.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos de UCLA crean un protector solar mineral que se adapta mejor a todos los tonos de piel
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchas personas evitan usar protector solar mineral porque a menudo deja una capa blanca o gris visible en la piel. Esto es especialmente cierto para quienes tienen tonos de piel más oscuros. El óxido de zinc, un ingrediente común en los protectores solares minerales, bloquea los rayos ultravioleta (UV) dañinos, pero puede agruparse y dispersar la luz, causando esa apariencia blanquecina.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de UCLA rediseñaron las partículas de óxido de zinc cambiando su forma a pequeñas estructuras con cuatro brazos llamadas tetrapodos. Esta nueva forma se distribuye mejor y reduce la capa blanca sin perder protección. Su fórmula de protector solar con SPF 30 fue probada en laboratorio y sobre la piel, mostrando un color más cálido que se ajusta mejor a los tonos de piel naturales que los protectores solares comunes.

**Impacto** – Este cambio podría hacer que el protector solar mineral sea más atractivo y fácil de usar para personas de todos los tonos de piel. Como el protector solar ayuda a prevenir el cáncer de piel, mejorar su apariencia puede motivar un uso más regular. Esto es importante porque las personas con piel más oscura suelen usar menos protector solar y a menudo reciben un diagnóstico de cáncer en etapas más avanzadas.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos continuarán las pruebas para asegurar que el nuevo protector solar sea seguro y efectivo para el uso diario. También estudian cómo estas nuevas partículas interactúan con las bacterias naturales de la piel. Colaboran con expertos en dermatología para acercar este producto al público.

**Resumen en una frase** – Al cambiar la forma de las partículas de óxido de zinc, los científicos de UCLA desarrollaron un protector solar mineral que protege la piel del sol sin dejar una capa blanca visible, facilitando la protección solar para todos.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of microscopic four-armed zinc oxide tetrapod particles gently floating and interlocking like delicate coral structures, casting a soft, natural glow over a smooth, gradient background that subtly transitions through diverse skin tone colors, symbolizing improved mineral sunscreen blending seamlessly with skin while blocking harmful ultraviolet rays.

articles.title: Huge study finds first genetic clues for borderline personality disorder
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 20 July 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-02220-1Largest genetic analysis of the condition so far finds 11 linked locations in the human genome.
articles.content:
Specific genetic variants can be linked to a particular condition by analysing the genomes of large groups of people with and without the disorder.Credit: Gregory Adams/Getty

Researchers have identified the first evidence of genetic risk factors associated with borderline personality disorder (BPD).

The analysis, published in Nature Genetics on 20 July1, is the largest genome-wide association study (GWAS) of the condition so far. It identified 11 associated locations (or loci) in the human genome, and within or near these broad areas were nine functional genes that seem to be linked to BPD.

The condition affects up to 2% of people in Western countries, and women are around three times more likely to be diagnosed with BPD than are men.

Genetic and environmental risk factors are thought to contribute to the risk of developing the condition. Yet, it has “been understudied from this perspective of psychiatric genetics”, says study co-author Fabian Streit, a psychologist at the Central Institute of Mental Health in Mannheim, Germany. Only one GWAS of the disorder had been published before, and it did not identify any significant variants associated with the condition2.

The latest findings suggest that “genetic approaches are going to be fruitful in the study of this disorder, as they have been in other disorders such as schizophrenia, depression and bipolar disorder”, says Andrew McIntosh, a psychiatrist who studies the genetics of such disorders at the University of Edinburgh, UK.

It is “an extraordinary step up from where we’ve been previously, with virtually no research being conducted on the condition at scale,” he adds.

Genetic factors

BPD is diagnosed on the basis of long-term patterns of emotional instability, cognitive and perceptual distortions and unstable relationships with others. People with the disorder often “have acute periods of crisis, which can sometimes lead to hospital admission,” explains McIntosh.

These factors have made it challenging to enrol people who have BPD in studies, he adds.

In the current work, Streit and his colleagues combined data from a total of 27 studies that recruited people with BPD or that analysed genetic and health records from biobanks. All participants were of European ancestry.

Huge genetic study reveals hidden links between psychiatric conditions

The team first examined more than six million genetic markers in two groups of people: 12,339 individuals with BPD and more than one million people who did not have the condition. The researchers found six sites on the genome that contained genetic variants that were associated with BPD. These variants remained significant in a replication analysis involving 685 people with BPD and 107,750 people without the disorder.

When the researchers combined both analyses, they identified five further loci on the genome that showed an association with BPD. These “five variants are still to be replicated in independent studies,” notes McIntosh.

Streit and his colleagues also estimated that the newly identified genetic variants account for about 17% of the inherited risk of BPD. This suggests it “is a disorder where genetics is pretty important, but it can never explain or paint the whole picture”, notes Streit.

Previous family and twin studies3,4 have suggested that the heritability of BPD is between 46% and 69%.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a major scientific breakthrough identifying the first genetic risk factors for borderline personality disorder (BPD) through the largest genome-wide association study to date. This discovery has significant potential to improve understanding, diagnosis, and treatment of a serious mental health condition affecting millions. The study's scale, clear findings, and implications for future research and patient care meet the criteria for inspiring good news with broad health impact.
articles.personality_title: Largest study finds first genetic links to borderline personality disorder
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Borderline personality disorder (BPD) is a mental health condition that affects about 2% of people in Western countries. It causes emotional instability and difficulty in relationships, and women are diagnosed about three times more often than men. Until now, scientists had little understanding of the genetic causes behind BPD.

**What happened** – On July 20, 2026, researchers published the biggest genetic study of BPD in the journal Nature Genetics. They looked at the DNA of over 12,000 people with BPD and more than a million without it, all of European background. The study found 11 specific spots in the human genome linked to BPD, including nine genes that may affect the disorder.

**Impact** – This is the first time scientists have found clear genetic clues related to BPD. It shows that genetics play an important role, explaining about 17% of the inherited risk. This discovery helps scientists understand the biology behind BPD and could lead to better ways to diagnose and treat it in the future.

**What’s next step** – Researchers will try to confirm these genetic findings with more studies and explore how these genes work in the brain. This could open the door to new medicines or therapies designed specifically for people with BPD.

**One-sentence takeaway** – For the first time, scientists have identified specific genetic factors linked to borderline personality disorder, marking a major step forward in understanding this complex condition.
articles.personality_title_fr: La plus grande étude révèle les premiers liens génétiques avec le trouble de la personnalité borderline
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le trouble de la personnalité borderline (TPB) est une maladie mentale qui touche environ 2 % des personnes dans les pays occidentaux. Il provoque une instabilité émotionnelle et des difficultés dans les relations, et les femmes sont diagnostiquées environ trois fois plus que les hommes. Jusqu’à présent, les scientifiques comprenaient peu les causes génétiques du TPB.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le 20 juillet 2026, des chercheurs ont publié la plus grande étude génétique sur le TPB dans la revue Nature Genetics. Ils ont analysé l’ADN de plus de 12 000 personnes atteintes de TPB et de plus d’un million sans, tous d’origine européenne. L’étude a identifié 11 endroits précis dans le génome liés au TPB, dont neuf gènes pouvant influencer ce trouble.

**Impact** – C’est la première fois que des indices génétiques clairs sont trouvés pour le TPB. Cela montre que la génétique joue un rôle important, expliquant environ 17 % du risque hérité. Cette découverte aide à mieux comprendre la biologie du TPB et pourrait améliorer son diagnostic et son traitement.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont confirmer ces résultats avec d’autres études et étudier comment ces gènes fonctionnent dans le cerveau. Cela pourrait mener à de nouveaux médicaments ou thérapies pour les personnes atteintes de TPB.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Pour la première fois, des facteurs génétiques spécifiques liés au trouble de la personnalité borderline ont été identifiés, marquant une avancée importante dans la compréhension de ce trouble complexe.
articles.personality_title_es: El mayor estudio encuentra los primeros vínculos genéticos con el trastorno límite de la personalidad
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El trastorno límite de la personalidad (TLP) es una condición mental que afecta a alrededor del 2 % de las personas en países occidentales. Causa inestabilidad emocional y problemas en las relaciones, y las mujeres son diagnosticadas unas tres veces más que los hombres. Hasta ahora, los científicos conocían poco sobre las causas genéticas del TLP.

**Qué pasó** – El 20 de julio de 2026, investigadores publicaron el estudio genético más grande sobre el TLP en la revista Nature Genetics. Analizaron el ADN de más de 12,000 personas con TLP y más de un millón sin la condición, todos de origen europeo. El estudio encontró 11 lugares específicos en el genoma relacionados con el TLP, incluyendo nueve genes que podrían influir en el trastorno.

**Impacto** – Es la primera vez que se encuentran pistas genéticas claras para el TLP. Esto muestra que la genética tiene un papel importante, explicando cerca del 17 % del riesgo heredado. Este descubrimiento ayuda a entender mejor la biología del TLP y podría mejorar su diagnóstico y tratamiento.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos intentarán confirmar estos hallazgos con más estudios y explorar cómo funcionan estos genes en el cerebro. Esto podría abrir la puerta a nuevos medicamentos o terapias para personas con TLP.

**Resumen en una frase** – Por primera vez, los científicos han identificado factores genéticos específicos vinculados al trastorno límite de la personalidad, marcando un avance importante en la comprensión de esta condición compleja.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of an abstract human DNA double helix gently intertwining with softly glowing puzzle pieces, set against a calm, neutral background, symbolizing the discovery of specific genetic loci linked to borderline personality disorder and the complexity of its hereditary nature.
